title: UC1 — Mission control's roster won't come online
description: Diagnose an ImagePullBackOff on the Artemis mission-control deployment with the help of the artemis-mission-control-debugger agent. Feel the manual diagnostic friction first; then watch the agent shortcut three commands into one synthesis.

steps.title: Apply the broken state
steps.explanation:
Mission control's deploy is hours late. Apply the manifests below to your vCluster — the **deployment.yaml** intentionally references a tag that never shipped (`mission-control:v999`), so this will reproduce the on-call ticket you're about to investigate.

The three files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`), then applied with a single `kubectl apply -f`.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
    kagent-workshop/scenario: imagepullbackoff

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc1
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc1
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          # Tag deliberately not published — produces ImagePullBackOff on first pull (FR-008).
          image: ghcr.io/kagent-workshop/mission-control:v999
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

steps.title: CLI baseline — listen to mission-control
steps.explanation:
Run the three commands below in order. Notice that you'll be holding three separate pieces of evidence — the pod's phase, the failing container's reason, and the recent events — and you'll have to piece them together yourself before you can name a root cause.

Give the kubelet ~30 s to attempt the pull before checking the pod state.

steps.title: Now ask the agent
steps.explanation:
Hand the same problem to **`artemis-mission-control-debugger`**. The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you used above (`get pod`, `describe pod`, `get events`) — the difference is that it correlates them in one synthesis and reports a single sentence of root cause.

The agent runs in the `artemis-uc1` namespace and uses the participant-scoped `artemis-llm` ModelConfig.

commands.label: Ask the agent
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-mission-control-debugger --namespace artemis-uc1 --task 'The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc1 namespace is not coming up. Diagnose it.'

steps.title: What did the agent do better?
steps.explanation:
In the CLI baseline you ran three commands and had to combine three pieces of state to reach a verdict: the pod's `Waiting` phase, the container's `ImagePullBackOff` reason, and the kubelet's repeated pull-failed events. The agent ran the same three tools and returned a single sentence of root cause: **the deploy targets `mission-control:v999`, which was never published**.

This is the simplest case of agent-vs-CLI value: same tools, same evidence, one synthesis instead of three. UC2 will scale this up to multi-resource correlation; UC3 to external observability; UC4 to multi-agent fan-out. For now, multiply this single-incident saving by the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week — that's the value the workshop is selling.
